what.title: What is incident management?
what.sub: Internal lifecycle for security incidents and exploited vulnerabilities
what.body:
The incident management feature provides a structured lifecycle for tracking security incidents from detection through to post-incident review. It satisfies the internal record-keeping requirements of CRA Article 14 (vulnerability and incident reporting) and supports NIS2 Article 23 (incident handling for essential and important entities).

Each incident moves through seven phases in a defined order:

  • Detection – a potential incident has been identified
  • Assessment – the incident is being evaluated for severity and scope
  • Containment – measures are in place to prevent further impact
  • Remediation – the root cause is being addressed
  • Recovery – normal operations are being restored
  • Review – lessons learned and preventive actions are documented
  • Closed – the incident is resolved and the record is retained
Incidents are tracked per product. Each incident has a severity rating (P1 critical through P4 low), a title, a description, and a full timeline of events. The timeline is immutable – entries cannot be edited or deleted after creation.

create.title: Log a new incident
create.sub: Create an incident record when a potential issue is identified
create.body:
Navigate to your product's Security tab group and select the Incidents tab. Click New Incident to create a record.

You will need to provide:

  • Title – a concise description (e.g. "Unauthorised access to staging API endpoint")
  • Description – what was observed, when it was detected, and the initial impact assessment
  • Severity – P1 (critical, active exploitation or data breach), P2 (high, potential for significant impact), P3 (medium, contained risk), or P4 (low, minimal impact)

The incident is created in the detection phase. A timeline entry is automatically recorded showing who created the incident and when.

P1 and P2 incidents involving actively exploited vulnerabilities may trigger ENISA reporting obligations under CRA Article 14. Assess whether escalation is required at the next phase.

Reversibility: incidents in the detection phase can be deleted if logged in error. Once the phase advances beyond detection, the record becomes permanent to preserve the audit trail.

assess.title: Assess severity and decide on escalation
assess.sub: Evaluate scope, determine if ENISA notification is required
assess.body:
Open the incident and update the phase to Assessment. The platform automatically logs the phase transition with a timestamp and the user who made the change.

During assessment, determine:

  • Scope – which users, systems, or data are affected
  • Root cause hypothesis – what is the suspected origin (vulnerability, misconfiguration, supply chain compromise)
  • ENISA trigger check – does this incident involve an actively exploited vulnerability or a severe security incident as defined by CRA Article 14(1)?

Add notes and evidence to the timeline using the Add Timeline Entry button. You can attach notes, actions taken, or evidence references. Each entry is timestamped and attributed to the user who created it.

If the incident involves an actively exploited vulnerability, you have 24 hours from awareness to submit an early warning to your designated CSIRT under CRA Article 14(2)(a). Use the Escalate to CRA Report button to create a linked Article 14 report immediately.

The escalation creates a CRA report (see the ENISA Reporting guide, ch5_01) that is linked to this incident. The report inherits the incident's product context, severity, and description.

remediate.title: Contain, remediate, and recover
remediate.sub: Fix the root cause and restore normal operations
remediate.body:
Progress the incident through three response phases as appropriate:

Containment – implement immediate measures to prevent further impact. Document each containment action as a timeline entry with the type "action taken". Examples: disabling a compromised endpoint, revoking access tokens, isolating an affected service.

Remediation – address the root cause. This might involve deploying a patch, updating a dependency, reconfiguring access controls, or rotating credentials. Record each remediation step in the timeline.

Recovery – verify that normal operations have been restored and that the remediation was effective. Confirm that monitoring is in place to detect recurrence.

If this incident has a linked CRA Article 14 report, the AI Copilot can help draft the notification and final report stages. Open the linked CRA report and use Draft with AI (prompt key: incident_report_draft). The Copilot uses the incident's product data, linked vulnerability findings, and timeline context to generate regulatory-quality report content. 5 calls per report per day.

Phase transitions are sequential – you cannot skip phases. Each transition records who made the change and when, creating an immutable compliance evidence trail per CRA Article 13(10).

review.title: Post-incident review
review.sub: Document lessons learned and preventive actions
review.body:
Move the incident to the Review phase after recovery is confirmed. This phase serves two regulatory purposes:

  • CRA Article 14(2)(c) – the final report must include a root cause analysis and corrective measures. The post-incident review provides this content.
  • NIS2 Article 23(4) – operators must provide a final report including a detailed description of the incident, the type of threat or root cause, and mitigation measures applied.

Document the following in timeline entries:

  • Root cause analysis – what happened and why
  • Corrective actions – what has been done to prevent recurrence
  • Process improvements – whether detection, escalation, or response procedures need updating
  • Stakeholder communication – whether users, partners, or authorities were notified
The complete incident timeline – from detection through review – constitutes compliance evidence under CRA Article 13(10). This record is retained for 10 years as part of the product's compliance documentation.

done.title: Close the incident
done.sub: Incident resolved and retained as compliance evidence
done.body:
Move the incident to the Closed phase when the post-incident review is complete. Closure is final – the incident cannot be reopened once closed.

The closed incident record includes:

  • Full immutable timeline from detection to closure
  • All phase transitions with timestamps and attributed users
  • Linked CRA Article 14 report (if escalated)
  • Severity classification and resolution summary

This record satisfies the evidence retention requirement of CRA Article 13(10), which mandates that compliance documentation be kept for at least 10 years after market placement or the support period, whichever is longer.

The incident summary page shows aggregated counts by phase and severity across all products. Use this dashboard to monitor open incidents and track resolution metrics for your organisation.

escalate.title: Escalate to ENISA (CRA Article 14)
escalate.sub: Create a linked CRA report for mandatory notification
escalate.body:
When an incident involves an actively exploited vulnerability or a severe security incident, CRA Article 14 requires notification to your designated CSIRT within strict deadlines:

  • Early warning – within 24 hours of becoming aware
  • Notification – within 72 hours with technical details
  • Final report – within 14 days (vulnerabilities) or 1 month (incidents)

Click Escalate to CRA Report on the incident detail page. This creates a CRA Article 14 report that is linked to the incident and pre-populated with the product context, severity, and description.

The linked CRA report appears in both the incident timeline and the ENISA Reporting page (ch5_01). Changes to either are reflected in both views.

The 24-hour early warning deadline starts from the moment you become aware of the actively exploited vulnerability or severe incident – not from when you create the incident record in CRANIS2. Log incidents promptly to preserve an accurate timeline.

copilot.title: AI Copilot for incident reports
copilot.sub: Draft ENISA notification content with AI assistance
copilot.body:
The AI Copilot can draft content for each of the three CRA Article 14 reporting stages. This capability is available from the linked CRA report (not directly from the incident page).

How to use:

  • Open the linked CRA report from the incident timeline
  • Navigate to the reporting stage you need to draft (early warning, notification, or final report)
  • Click Draft with AI
  • Review the generated content, edit as needed, and confirm

Copilot specification:

  • Prompt key: incident_report_draft
  • Rate limit: 5 calls per report per day
  • Model: Claude Sonnet
  • Lifecycle: per-incident, per-stage (typically used once per stage)
  • Admin customisation: /admin/copilot
The Copilot drafts are most effective when the incident has detailed timeline entries. Add notes about scope, root cause, containment actions, and affected systems before triggering the AI draft – the more context available, the more substantive the generated report content.
